title: Stop training people for yesterday’s jobs: AI demands we redesign work
full_text:
AI has moved quickly from a supporting tool to something closer to a general-purpose capability. In the next 12 to 24 months, that shift will start to show up less in new job titles and more in how work is structured and rewarded. We have moved from pilots and productivity initiatives to workflow redesign and enterprise transformation.
The default response has been to talk about skills. Skills matter, but they are downstream of design. As AI reshapes tasks inside and across roles, the core leadership question becomes: what work should still exist in its current form, and what should be redesigned around new capability?
This was a key discussion at Deloitte CONNECT 2026, a forum where senior leaders convened to discuss evolving trends in business and technology.
The event was a reality check on the operating decisions leaders have been avoiding, which processes to redesign first, what guardrails are non-negotiable, and how to bring people along so adoption does not stall.
Work redesign will determine whether AI lifts productivity and creates new business value
The collective message was clear: it is essential to undertake redesign efforts prior to implementing reskilling initiatives. Providing training without first addressing process design may lead to more inefficiency, not less.
Elise Sharpley is Partner and Head of Consumer Industry at Deloitte Australia
Speaking at CONNECT, Ben Hancock, Head of AI, ANZ at NiCE, explained: “When we think about AI agents, we run towards existing processes and think wouldn’t it be great if humans didn’t have to do this. What we’re missing is, do these workflows need to exist in the way that they currently exist? Are we just automating an inefficient process or an unnecessary process?”
Organisations are significantly more likely to surpass their AI return on investment when they focus on effective work design. The overall benefits are determined as much by operating model choices and adoption as by technological advancements.
And once work is redesigned, the human contribution comes into sharper focus. As routine cognitive tasks are automated, value shifts toward what people do best, such as applying judgment or generating ideas in meaning-rich environments.
In practice, work redesign can be as simple as removing steps that only exist because information used to be hard to find. If an agent can assemble a brief, draft customer correspondence, or surface policy guidance in seconds, then the process should shrink around that reality.
Capability, however, is not just about tool proficiency. It is about judgement, specifically knowing what to automate, what to escalate and what outcome is required. Yet many organisations remain stuck in a familiar pattern where they are treating AI as a traditional technology rollout rather than a work transformation program.
As this column has noted recently, that’s putting the cart before the horse. Deloitte research shows organisations are twice as likely to exceed their AI ROI expectations when they put work design first. This shift has obvious implications for Australia’s education and training system too. Digital skills do not have the same currency they once held. Katrina Troughton, Vice President and Managing Director for Adobe ANZ and Deputy Chairperson for TAFE NSW, captures the urgency: “The half-life of a digital skill these days is probably under two years.”
Formal education remains critical, but pathways must become more flexible and modular, enabling continuous learning as work evolves.
Deloitte’s Human Capital Trends research suggests most organisations recognise culture as critical for AI adoption, but far fewer are actively shaping how humans and machines work together. In the next year, the gap will show up as uneven uptake, shadow usage and stalled pilots.
Frank Fillmann, EVP & General Manager at Salesforce Australia and New Zealand, makes a similar point: “If you don’t provide guardrails, people will bring personal AI in with good intent and your intellectual property just goes out. You have to provide it in a safe environment so you can scale it from a trusted place.”
There is also a broader opportunity that should not be overlooked. AI-enabled work redesign creates the conditions for more inclusive workforce participation.
As Angela Colantuono, President and Managing Director for SAP Australia and New Zealand, noted: “For most organisations, the answer isn’t hiring AI skills — it’s training the people they already trust. Plus, AI is increasingly the tool that finds those people, surfacing hidden capabilities within existing teams that traditional talent processes would never have seen.”
So, what should leaders do next? Stop measuring readiness by how many people have been trained and start measuring how much work has been redesigned. Treat AI not as a training program supported by technology, but as a work design transformation supported by continuous learning.
The organisations that move first will not simply deploy more AI. They will design better work, aligning humans and machines, embedding learning into the flow of work and building systems that can evolve as fast as the technology itself.
This article is part one of a three-part series based on Deloitte CONNECT.
Elise Sharpley is Partner and Head of Consumer Industry at Deloitte Australia. This article is part one of a three-part series based on Deloitte CONNECT.

summary: The shift towards AI as a general-purpose capability necessitates a redesign of work processes before implementing reskilling initiatives to enhance productivity and business value.
author: Elise Sharpley
heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: AI is transforming work structures and requires redesign rather than just reskilling.
Effective work design significantly increases the likelihood of surpassing AI return on investment.
Organisations must focus on continuous learning and adapting processes to integrate AI effectively.
relevance: Understanding the implications of AI on work processes is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals to remain competitive and enhance operational efficiency.
